title: Every object has a prototype
instruction:
Every JavaScript object has a hidden link to another object — its **prototype**. When you access a property that doesn't exist on the object, JavaScript looks up the prototype chain.

That's why `[].push()` works — arrays don't each have their own push method. They all share one from `Array.prototype`.

Run this.
successMessage: push lives on Array.prototype, not on each array. All arrays SHARE one push method through the prototype chain. This is why JavaScript is memory-efficient — methods aren't copied.

title: The prototype chain lookup
instruction:
When you access `obj.property`, JavaScript looks:
1. On `obj` itself
2. On `obj.__proto__` (obj's prototype)
3. On `obj.__proto__.__proto__` (prototype's prototype)
4. All the way to `null` — then returns `undefined`

Run this to see the chain in action.
successMessage: name is dog's own property. speak lives on Animal.prototype. When you call dog.speak(), JS looks on dog (not found), then on Animal.prototype (found!) and calls it.

title: Classes are prototype sugar
instruction:
ES6 class syntax compiles down to prototype-based code. The class version and the prototype version are identical under the hood.

Run both and compare.
successMessage: typeof PersonClass === 'function' — classes are functions! The class keyword is syntactic sugar. Understanding this is key to debugging weird class-related bugs.

title: Object.create — manual prototype chain
instruction:
`Object.create(proto)` creates a new object with `proto` as its prototype — without a constructor function.

This is the purest form of prototypal inheritance in JavaScript.
successMessage: dog was created with animal as its prototype directly. No classes, no constructors. Object.create is the foundational prototype tool — classes are built on top of it.

title: hasOwnProperty vs in
instruction:
Two ways to check if a property exists:

- `hasOwnProperty('key')` — only on the object itself (not prototype)
- `'key' in obj` — includes inherited properties from the prototype chain

This matters when looping — `for...in` includes prototype properties!
successMessage: for...in + hasOwnProperty is the safe pattern for looping over object keys. Modern code often uses Object.keys() (own only) instead to avoid this entirely.

title: Extending built-in prototypes — why NOT to
instruction:
You CAN add methods to built-in prototypes — but you almost never should:

```js
Array.prototype.first = function() { return this[0]; };
[1, 2, 3].first(); // works — but bad!
```

Why is it bad? If two libraries both add `Array.prototype.first`, the second overwrites the first. Chaos.

Run the example. Then notice why prototype pollution is dangerous.
successMessage: See 'sum' appearing in the for...in loop? That's prototype pollution. Stick to utility functions or wrapper classes instead of extending built-ins.

title: Object.assign vs spread
instruction:
`Object.assign(target, ...sources)` copies properties from sources to target.
`{...obj}` spread does the same but creates a new object.

Both are **shallow copies** — nested objects are still shared references!

See the shallow copy gotcha.
successMessage: Shallow copy: nested objects are shared. structuredClone() does a true deep copy — use it for nested data. JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(obj)) is the old workaround (but loses functions, dates).

title: Build a prototype-based inheritance chain
instruction:
Final challenge — using the prototype approach (no classes).

Build a three-level chain: `Vehicle → Car → ElectricCar`.
- Vehicle: `make`, `model`, `start()` method
- Car: inherits Vehicle, adds `fuelType = 'gasoline'`, overrides `start()`
- ElectricCar: inherits Car, adds `batteryRange`, overrides `start()` to mention silent operation

Use `Object.create` for the prototype chain.
successMessage: Three levels of prototype inheritance without a single class keyword. You just did what JavaScript has always done under the hood. Chapter 14 done — prototypes demystified!
